title: ‘Silent attrition’ looms as AI reshapes client behaviour
full_text:
Wealth managers are being warned they could be sidelined by clients who have embraced AI independently and are using it for their investment decisions.
The rise of AI has triggered widely varying responses across and within every sector, with some viewing the technology as an opportunity enabler while others are fretting over what it will mean for their future employment or business, and the wealth industry is no exception.
Based on assessments of professional insights from across the industry, EY’s Global Wealth Management Industry Report uses a dynamic Strategy Radar to indicate wealth professionals need to get on board with AI, or risk being left behind.
“Wealthy individuals increasingly view external AI tools as the first stop for wealth-related guidance, forming advisory habits outside wealth management firms. This matters profoundly, and not just because public AI is getting better at handling everyday questions.”
Given the widespread accessibility of AI as well as the technology’s ever-increasing presence in more complex aspects of peoples’ lives – such as their finances – EY said that, in order to avoid losing clients, the industry must act before client-controlled AI overtakes them.
“Wealth managers face a narrowing window to integrate AI into their advisory offerings in order to retain clients shifting towards self-direction — before client-controlled AI begins to mediate those relationships.
“Self-directed investable assets are predicted to continue the strong growth of the last 20 years. In our view, this trend is likely to accelerate as AI reduces barriers to self-direct, pushing mature markets towards a potential equilibrium of about 35 per cent fully and 50 per cent partially self-directed clients in coming years,” the report said.
“Self-direction typically follows a consistent pattern. Self-reliant, cost-sensitive investors with perceived above-average financial literacy begin shifting some assets or cash inflows into execution-only offerings. Over time, silent attrition accelerates as more assets migrate to self-direction.”
Adding to this challenge is the influx of AI-enabled platforms offering wealth management skills which EY warned could further drive revenue erosion by shifting capital to lower-fee products.
Meanwhile, standard investment guidance is set to become “harder to defend at a premium” leading to a concentration of ‘human-priced advice’ in areas where judgement, accountability and complexity remain decisive.
While these events may seem somewhat inevitable, the report suggested that wealth professions need to focus on early intervention for clients they consider at risk of silent asset leakage while turning more toward clients open to maintaining hybrid mandates within existing relationships.
At the same time, clients’ increased comfort in using AI for basic financial guidance is slowly eroding their reliance on advisers, and while the technology has been highly unreliable in the past, it is increasingly becoming better at handling everyday questions.
“Each off-platform interaction also weakens firms’ visibility of client intent, needs and behaviour. Wealth managers not only risk advice substitution, but loss of relevance – and displacement from the start of the advisory journey.”
Accepting that clients will inevitably turn to AI, the battle now is between financial services providers and publicly-available AI solutions for who will “earn the trust and data access to make it matter”.
“With more clients testing AI for wealth guidance, banks face a major threat of disintermediation in early stages of client journeys. Those that deliver a reliable, auditable, human-backed AI advisor experience will retain mandates, deepen relevance and outperform purely digital competitors.
“Winning firms will close the proactive advice gap quickly, keep more of the advisory journey inside the bank, and make human escalation frictionless.”
author: Shy-Ann Arkinstall
heading: Data Science and AI
page_title: ‘Silent attrition’ looms as AI reshapes client behaviour - Money Management
